summary: Thales Alenia Space, a leading European satellite manufacturer and systems integrator, has engaged with space-based solar power concept studies contributing industrial architecture expertise and spacecraft systems engineering perspective to the European SBSP feasibility discourse. Thales Alenia Space's engagement occurs within the ESA SOLARIS framework and European industrial SBSP assessment activities. The company's expertise in large telecommunications and science spacecraft, structural systems, and power management is directly relevant to SBSP system design challenges. These activities are concept study and assessment phase, not a funded SBSP product development programme.
institutional_context: Thales Alenia Space is a joint venture between Thales and Leonardo, operating as one of Europe's major space systems integrators. The company has significant experience in large GEO telecommunications platforms, space station modules (including International Space Station elements), and complex space system integration. These capabilities are directly applicable to the structural and systems integration challenges of SBSP.
sbsp_relevance: Thales Alenia Space's SBSP concept contributions are significant as the involvement of a company with hands-on experience in large space structure integration. Its expertise in space station module manufacturing and GEO platform systems gives it particularly relevant industrial perspective on the in-space assembly and large structure challenges central to SBSP feasibility.
technology_relationship: Thales Alenia Space SBSP studies engage with modular spacecraft architecture, large space structure dynamics, in-space assembly, spacecraft thermal control, and solar array structural design as primary technology domains within the company's existing industrial capability.
feasibility_boundary: Thales Alenia Space SBSP activities are concept study and industrial assessment phase. No Thales Alenia Space SBSP product development programme has been confirmed. Industrial concept contributions are distinct from development commitments.
claim_boundary: Thales Alenia Space SBSP concept contributions should be presented as industrial study-phase inputs, not as development commitments. The company's space station and GEO platform experience is relevant context but does not imply readiness to build an SBSP system.
source_footer: Based on publicly available Thales Alenia Space communications, ESA programme documentation referencing Thales Alenia participation, and published European SBSP study reports. All activities are described at concept study and assessment phase.
seoTitle: Thales Alenia Space SBSP Concepts — European Space Solar Power Industry
seoDescription: Thales Alenia Space contributes space systems integration expertise and large structure experience to European space-based solar power concept studies within the ESA SOLARIS framework.

title: ITU Spectrum Regulatory Framework for Space Power Systems
summary: The International Telecommunication Union's regulatory framework for spectrum allocation is a critical institutional constraint for any operational space-based solar power system. SBSP microwave wireless power transmission requires allocation of specific frequency bands under international agreement, and the ITU's processes for coordinating spectrum use among nations and services are directly relevant to SBSP deployment planning. ITU discussions on spectrum for space power applications represent an emerging regulatory frontier as national SBSP programmes advance from research to potential demonstration phases. No ITU spectrum allocation specifically for SBSP operational systems has yet been established.
institutional_context: The ITU is the United Nations specialised agency for information and communication technologies, responsible for coordinating the global use of the radio spectrum and satellite orbits. ITU Radio Regulations govern spectrum allocation and coordination between member states. Any operational SBSP system using microwave wireless power transmission would require ITU-coordinated spectrum allocation in the frequency bands used for power beaming, creating a regulatory pathway that must be engaged well in advance of operational deployment.
sbsp_relevance: ITU spectrum regulation is a non-technical but critical feasibility constraint for operational SBSP. The need to secure internationally coordinated spectrum allocation for microwave power beaming frequencies represents a regulatory hurdle that is independent of technology readiness and must be addressed through ITU processes that operate on long timescales. ITU engagement is therefore a leading indicator of the institutional seriousness of national SBSP programmes.
technology_relationship: ITU spectrum regulation is most directly relevant to microwave power transmission and phased array transmitter systems as the technology domains whose operational frequency requirements create the regulatory interface with ITU processes.
feasibility_boundary: No ITU spectrum allocation specifically for SBSP operational power beaming has been established. Spectrum regulatory pathway for SBSP remains to be defined through ITU processes that have not yet been formally initiated for this application.
claim_boundary: ITU regulatory engagement with SBSP should not be presented as spectrum clearance or regulatory approval for SBSP operations. ITU processes are long-lead regulatory requirements, not endorsements. The absence of established spectrum allocation is a genuine feasibility constraint for operational SBSP deployment.
source_footer: Based on publicly available ITU Radio Regulations, ITU documentation on frequency allocation processes, and published SBSP research literature addressing regulatory constraints. All regulatory characterisations reflect current ITU processes and publicly documented spectrum allocation status.
seoTitle: ITU Spectrum Regulation for SBSP — Space Power Systems Frequency Policy
seoDescription: ITU spectrum regulatory frameworks are a critical non-technical constraint for space-based solar power, requiring internationally coordinated frequency allocation for microwave power beaming.

title: Peter Glaser 1968 Space Solar Power Proposal
summary: Peter Glaser's 1968 proposal, published in Science magazine, is the foundational concept document for space-based solar power. Glaser proposed collecting solar energy in geostationary orbit using large photovoltaic arrays and transmitting the energy to Earth via microwave wireless power transmission to ground-based receiving antennas. The proposal identified the core architectural elements — orbital solar collection, wireless power transmission, and ground reception — that define all subsequent SBSP concepts. Glaser's 1968 paper is the origin point of the SBSP field and is universally cited as the foundational reference in international SBSP research literature. The proposal was conceptual and no technology existed at the time to implement it.
institutional_context: Peter Glaser was a scientist at Arthur D. Little and later associated with the Smithsonian Institution. His 1968 Science paper 'Power from the Sun: Its Future' introduced the satellite solar power station concept and stimulated subsequent US government interest that led to the 1979 NASA reference system study. Glaser's proposal emerged from 1960s thinking about long-range energy solutions and was informed by early space programme achievements that made geostationary orbit a practical operational environment.
sbsp_relevance: Glaser's 1968 proposal is the essential historical origin of the SBSP field. All subsequent SBSP architectural concepts, feasibility studies, and national programmes trace their intellectual lineage to Glaser's foundational concept. Understanding Glaser's original proposal is necessary for contextualising the subsequent history of SBSP research, including the reasons why the concept attracted NASA study in the 1970s and why it was not pursued further at that time.
technology_relationship: Glaser's 1968 proposal conceptually identified the three primary technology subsystems of SBSP: solar collection (photovoltaics), wireless power transmission (microwave beaming), and ground reception (rectenna). None of these subsystems existed at operational scale in 1968, and Glaser's proposal was a conceptual forward projection, not an engineering design based on demonstrated technology.
feasibility_boundary: Glaser's 1968 proposal was a conceptual proposal, not an engineering design or feasibility study. The technologies required for SBSP did not exist at operational scale in 1968 and remain below operational readiness today. The proposal's significance is conceptual and historical, not as evidence of technical feasibility at the time of publication.
claim_boundary: Glaser's 1968 proposal should be cited as the foundational historical concept for SBSP, not as evidence that SBSP was technically feasible in 1968 or that subsequent progress has resolved the challenges Glaser identified. The proposal identified the concept; it did not validate the technology or the economics.
source_footer: Based on Peter Glaser's publicly available 1968 Science paper 'Power from the Sun: Its Future', subsequent historical accounts of SBSP programme history, and the 1979 NASA reference system study's citation of Glaser's foundational work.
seoTitle: Peter Glaser 1968 SBSP Proposal — Foundational Space Solar Power Concept
seoDescription: Peter Glaser's 1968 Science paper introduced the satellite solar power station concept, establishing the foundational architecture for all subsequent space-based solar power research.
